ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general pasta food product class; no specific pesto pasta with pine nuts entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers cooked pasta as a category. No specific pesto pasta sub-entry exists; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 229953001 covers pasta as a food concept; no specific pesto pasta with pine nuts entry exists. Closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of pasta with pine nut basil pesto is approximately 300 g, comprising cooked pasta tossed with pesto sauce and garnished with additional pine nuts.

nutrient_source.title: Pasta, cooked, enriched, without added salt (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy 20420) combined with Pesto sauce, commercially prepared (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy 06408) and Pine nuts, dried (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy 12147), proportionally weighted for a typical pesto pasta dish
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for pesto pasta with pine nuts as a composite dish. Values estimated by proportional weighting: approximately 65% cooked pasta (USDA SR Legacy 20420), 25% basil pesto sauce (USDA SR Legacy 06408), and 10% pine nuts (USDA SR Legacy 12147). Energy and macros cross-checked against USDA Foundation Foods composite data for pasta dishes. This is a medium-confidence estimate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; pasta (spaghetti, white, boiled 10–15 min, GI ~44)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pesto pasta with pine nuts as a composite dish. GI of 44 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white pasta (spaghetti, boiled 10–15 min), which is the primary carbohydrate component. The addition of pesto (fat and protein) would further lower the effective GI; therefore GI 44 is a conservative upper-bound estimate. GL per serving calculated as (44 × 82.5 g carbohydrate in 300 g serving) / 100 = 36.3. gi_category is low (GI ≤ 55).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from pesto sauce and Parmesan cheese
hypertension.guidance: A 300 g serving provides approximately 630 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size to manage daily sodium intake below 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from refined pasta carbohydrates
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 44 and a glycemic load of ~36 per standard serving, this dish has a low GI but a moderate-to-high glycemic load; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a smaller portion (150–200 g) and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Predominantly unsaturated fat from olive oil and pine nuts
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The fat profile is dominated by monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fats from olive oil and pine nuts (~7.2 g per 100 g), with low saturated fat (~1.8 g per 100 g), which is consistent with a Mediterranean dietary pattern associated with reduced cardiovascular risk; sodium should still be monitored.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pasta with Pine Nuts and Basil Pesto is a classic Italian pasta dish originating from the Ligurian region of Italy, where traditional pesto alla Genovese is made by blending fresh basil leaves, pine nuts, garlic, Parmesan cheese, and extra-virgin olive oil. The dish is prepared by boiling pasta — typically spaghetti, linguine, or penne — until al dente, then tossing it with the freshly made or commercially prepared pesto sauce and topping with additional toasted pine nuts. The result is a vibrant green, aromatic dish with a rich, herbaceous flavour, a slightly nutty texture from the pine nuts, and a creamy mouthfeel from the emulsified olive oil and cheese. In Singapore, this dish is commonly served in Western and global-cuisine restaurants as a main course, priced in the mid-range segment. Nutritionally, it provides moderate energy (~220 kcal per 100 g), a meaningful amount of carbohydrate from the pasta (~27.5 g per 100 g), and a favourable unsaturated fat profile from olive oil and pine nuts. The glycemic index is low (~44), making it a better pasta choice for blood glucose management compared to high-GI refined grain dishes, though the glycemic load per full serving remains moderate due to portion size. Clinically, the dish is relevant for individuals monitoring sodium (from pesto and Parmesan), and those with Type 2 Diabetes should moderate portion size. It is suitable for vegetarians but not vegans due to Parmesan cheese, and contains gluten and tree nut allergens.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for pesto pasta with pine nuts as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by proportional weighting of three USDA SR Legacy entries (cooked pasta, pesto sauce, pine nuts). GI is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database for white pasta as the closest analogous carbohydrate source. Medium confidence is appropriate for a composite estimation.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entries used for this composite estimation. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g per 100 g; this is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for the component ingredients (pasta, pesto, pine nuts) — no industrial trans fats are present in this dish. Nutrient values are composite estimates weighted at approximately 65% cooked pasta (USDA SR Legacy 20420), 25% basil pesto sauce (USDA SR Legacy 06408), and 10% pine nuts (USDA SR Legacy 12147). Actual restaurant values may vary depending on pasta type, pesto recipe, and portion size. The GI of 44 is a conservative upper-bound estimate based on white pasta; the effective GI of the composite dish with added fat and protein from pesto is likely lower. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (44 × 82.5 g carbohydrate in 300 g serving) / 100 = 36.3. The dish is served at Flavours by Saute - Funan on field survey data at SGD 15.50. Halal status is unconfirmed as Parmesan cheese may use animal rennet; is_halal is set to false by default pending confirmation.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built full composite record from USDA SR Legacy sources. Added multilingual names for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi per Singapore-served dish rule. Documented vitamin_d_mcg = -1 sentinel value in notes. Documented trans_fat_g = 0.0 confirmed zero in notes. Set GI to 44 from University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for white pasta. Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases consistency. Set gi_category to 'low' per GI ≤ 55 threshold rule. All hard gates and Q1–Q8 pass on first iteration.
